query: effectiveness of primaquine vs chloroquine in treating malaria

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated P. vivax infections in chloroquine-resistant regions (Indonesia, Papua New Guinea) get treated with quinine sulfate as per above plus either doxycycline, primaquine, or tafenoquine as per above; or atovaquone-proguanil as per above plus either primaquine or tafenoquine; or mefloquine as per above plus either primaquine or tafenoquine as per above.

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated P. vivax or P. ovale infections in chloroquine-sensitive regions receive treatment with chloroquine phosphate or hydroxychloroquine as per above, plus either primaquine phosphate 30 mg (pediatric: 0.5 mg/kg) daily for 14 days or tafenoquine 300 mg once (same in children older than 16 years).

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated infections with any species in pregnant women in chloroquine-sensitive regions require treatment with chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine as per above.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmodium vivax Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In regions where P. vivax is chloroquine resistant the CDC offers three possible treatment options: atovaquone-proguanil, quinine plus either tetracycline or doxycycline or mefloquine. Providers should select one of these three options to combine with primaquine.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Uncomplicated Falciparum Malaria
retrieved_docs.content: There are some other treatment options, although not as effective as ACTs, such as atovaquone-proguanil, quinine sulfate plus doxycycline, tetracycline, or clindamycin, and mefloquine. For chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum infections (including

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria Prophylaxis -- Function -- Chemoprophylaxis Choices
retrieved_docs.content: Malarial chemoprophylaxis functions by targeting the liver schizont, blood schizont, or hypnozoite stages of the plasmodium life cycle. The three most commonly prescribed medications for chemoprophylaxis are atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, and mefloquine. Studies examining the efficacy of the medications have found them all to be equally effective in the prevention of malaria in short-term travelers, but atovaquone-proguanil and doxycycline appear to have the fewest side effects. For long term travelers, studies suggest that adherence is lower in young travelers and those on mefloquine.

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated P. falciparum infections in chloroquine-resistant or unknown regions are treated with atovaquone-proguanil 250 mg/100 mg 4 tabs (pediatric: varied weight-based dosing, 6.5 mg/25 mg tabs) daily for 4 days; or artemether-lumefantrine 20 mg/120 mg 4 tabs (pediatric: varied weight-based tabs) at initial dose, then 8 hours later, then twice daily for 2 days; or quinine sulfate 542 mg (pediatric: 8.3 mg/kg) three times daily for 3 days (7 days if in Southeast Asia) plus either doxycycline 100 mg daily for 7 days (pediatrics 2.2 mg/kg every 12 hours), or tetracycline 250 mg daily for 7 days (pediatric: 25 mg/kg/day divided four times daily for 7 days), or clindamycin 20 mg/kg/day divided three times daily for 7 days (pediatric: same); or mefloquine 684 mg (pediatric: 13.7 mg/kg) loading dose followed by 456 mg (pediatric: 9.1 mg/kg) every 6 to 12 hours for total of 1250 mg (pediatric total: 25 mg/kg).

retrieved_docs.content: pregnant women), the drug of choice is chloroquine. However, any of the drug choices listed above for chloroquine-resistant strains can be used. Treatment options are the same for the pediatric population, with doses adjusted by the patient’s weight.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Uncomplicated Falciparum Malaria
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated falciparum malaria should be treated with one of the artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACT). Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) is highly effective due to its effect on a broader range of parasite life cycles, causing faster parasite clearance and is therefore considered a drug of choice for uncomplicated malaria. [24] The duration of ACT treatment is 3 days. ACTs are also recommended for pregnant women in the second and third trimester but should be used during the first trimester only if other treatment options are not available. During the first trimester, a combination of quinine and clindamycin should be prescribed. In low transmission areas, gametocytocidal therapy (e.g., primaquine) is added to ACTs to reduce transmission potential (except pregnant women, infants younger than 6 months and women breastfeeding infants younger than 6 months). [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment involves combination therapy targeting both the hepatic and erythrocytic forms. [17] The chief antimalarials are chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, primaquine, artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT), and atovaquone-proguanil. Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine are synthetic forms of quinine. [18] [19] They disrupt the erythrocytic stage by interfering with parasitic hemoglobin metabolism and increasing intracellular pH. [18] [19] They generally require two days of treatment, allowing for better tolerance and shorter admissions. [2] However, chloroquine may enhance gametogenesis, contributing to resistance, which is a concern, particularly in South Asia. [17] Primaquine is a hypnozointocidal agent added for P. vivax or P. ovale infection for the eradication of liver parasites and the prevention of dormancy and relapse. [2] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antimalarials - chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine

retrieved_docs.title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The drug of choice to treat malaria is dependent on the Plasmodium species, the geographic region of the infecting species, and the severity of the patient’s infection. [2] Chloroquine is the drug of choice to treat uncomplicated malaria caused by all Plasmodium species ( P. vivax, P. malariae, P. ovale, and P. knowlesi ) except P. falciparum , which has become increasingly resistant. [3] Amodiaquine (discontinued in USA) can be used in combination with artesunate to treat chloroquine-resistant uncomplicated P. falciparum. [3] An atovaquone-proguanil combination or an artemether-lumefantrine combination may also be a choice for first-line treatment for chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum . In pregnancy, oral quinine is indicated, and parenteral quinine(discontinued in USA) is useful for the treatment of severe malaria. Primaquine, mefloquine, atovaquone-proguanil, and doxycycline are indicated for chemoprophylaxis. [3] Antibabesial agents:

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated infections with any species in pregnant women in chloroquine-resistant regions are treated with quinine sulfate as per above plus either clindamycin or mefloquine as per above in the first, second, or third trimesters; or artemether-lumefantrine as per above in only the second and third trimesters.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Falciparum Malaria
retrieved_docs.content: If artesunate is not available, the recommended drug is artemether in preference to quinine. After the complete course of a 24 hour intravenous artesunate, a regimen of a follow-up drug should be completed. A full 3-day regimen of ACT is recommended. In returning traveler, the follow-on antimalarial medication needs to be other than the antimalarial medication taken for prophylaxis. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria Prophylaxis -- Function -- Chemoprophylaxis Choices
retrieved_docs.content: Primaquine: Primaquine targets liver hypnozoites and can be used as prophylaxis in areas with only P. vivax . Travelers begin the medication two days before travel and take it daily until seven days after return. Primaquine is more common as presumptive antirelapse therapy (PART), where it is taken daily upon returning from a P. vivax or P. ovale endemic area in the final 14 days of post-travel treatment. The exception is when atovaquone-proguanil was used for primary prevention, in which case primaquine gets administered along with the final seven days of the primary prevention drug and for an additional seven days. Providers must test travelers for glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency before prescribing the medication as it can cause fatal hemolytic anemia. Side effects consist mostly of gastrointestinal upset. Prophylaxis with primaquine is currently an off-label recommendation from the CDC. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Falciparum Malaria
retrieved_docs.content: Intravenous artesunate is currently not approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) or commercially available in the U.S. but is available from CDC under an expanded-access investigational drug (IND) protocol. It is prepositioned at distribution sites throughout the USA and readily available after contacting CDC. Since severe malaria can progress rapidly, CDC offers guidance on oral treatment that can be used while waiting for IV artesunate to be delivered. They recommend interim treatment with oral artemether-lumefantrine (preferably), atovaquone-proguanil, quinine sulfate, or mefloquine. If a patient can not tolerate oral medications, administration after an antiemetic or via nasogastric tube should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite responsible for malaria, is treated with a broad spectrum of antimalarial agents that are predominantly formulated as oral tablets. Combination tablets include artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg, atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg, and a lower‑strength atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg. Single‑agent antimalarial tablets comprise chloroquine phosphate (250 mg and 500 mg), hydroxychloroquine sulfate (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), mefloquine hydrochloride (250 mg), primaquine phosphate (26.3 mg), pyrimethamine (25 mg), sulfadoxine, tafenoquine (100 mg and 150 mg), and the identified agent acedapsone. Additional antimalarial products mentioned are chinidin and tafenoquine. All of these medications share the tablet dosage form, providing a standardized route of administration that facilitates dosing flexibility and patient adherence. The diversity of chemical structures and mechanisms of action among these tablets enables clinicians to tailor malaria prophylaxis and therapy to individual patient needs, including considerations of resistance patterns, severity of infection, and safety profiles. The tablet dosage form is also employed across other therapeutic classes, exemplified by griseofulvin 500 mg (an antifungal) and hydrocortisone tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg), underscoring the widespread utility of oral tablets in modern pharmacotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 893
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial agents. Antimalarials constitute a therapeutic class that includes a variety of oral tablet formulations used to treat or prevent Plasmodium infection. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial, and the antimalarial class further comprises artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablets, atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg oral tablets, chinidin, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablets, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg oral tablets, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg oral tablets, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablets, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 150 mg oral tablets. Each of these agents works through distinct mechanisms to inhibit the growth, replication, or survival of Plasmodium species, thereby providing clinicians with multiple options for effective malaria management and prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy, which is largely delivered in oral tablet form. The antimalarial class includes a wide range of agents such as acedapsone; artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg tablets; atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg tablets and the lower‑strength atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg tablets that combine a mitochondrial electron‑transport inhibitor with a dihydrofolate‑reductase antagonist; chloroquine phosphate tablets (250 mg and 500 mg); hydroxychloroquine sulfate tablets (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), both 4‑aminoquinoline compounds active against blood‑stage parasites; mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg tablets; primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg tablets targeting hepatic stages; pyrimethamine 25 mg tablets; and tafenoquine tablets (100 mg and 150 mg) that interfere with folate metabolism or mitochondrial function. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy. A wide range of antimalarial agents are formulated as oral tablets, providing convenient dosing and supporting adherence to treatment regimens. Tablet formulations include artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg, atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg, atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg and 500 mg, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg and 400 mg, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg, pyrimethamine 25 mg, tafenoquine 100 mg and 150 mg, as well as the natural product chinidin and sulfadoxin. Acedapsone is classified as an antimalarial and is used for treatment and prevention of Plasmodium infection. Although trimethoprim + sulfamethoxazole is occasionally listed among antimalarials, it is primarily an antibacterial combination and represents a misclassification. In addition to antimalarials, other therapeutic agents are available as oral tablets, such as the antifungal griseofulvin 500 mg and the corticosteroids hydrocortisone 5 mg, 10 mg and 20 mg, illustrating the broad use of the tablet dosage form across drug classes. Recognizing the specific agents, their tablet strengths, mechanisms of action, and appropriate indications is essential for optimal drug selection, dosing, management of malaria, and minimization of drug‑resistance risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 915
retrieved_docs.content: Artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone 20 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg oral tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet, and pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet are all formulated as tablets for oral administration. This shared dose form provides a familiar and convenient vehicle for a diverse group of therapeutic agents—including antimalarials, an antifungal, a corticosteroid, and an antiparasitic—facilitating prescribing, simplifying medication management, and supporting patient adherence. The specific milligram strengths listed for each product reflect the dosing requirements of their respective indications, allowing precise titration and appropriate therapeutic effect while maintaining the advantages of a tablet formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 232
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a diverse pharmacologic class known as antimalarials, all of which are commonly formulated as oral tablets to promote standardized prescribing and patient adherence. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial agent. Artemisinin‑based combination therapy is represented by a fixed‑dose tablet containing artemether 20 mg and lumefantrine 120 mg, which targets the blood stages of Plasmodium species. Atovaquone‑proguanil is available in two tablet strengths—250 mg/100 mg and 62.5 mg/25 mg—providing both treatment and prophylaxis by inhibiting mitochondrial electron transport (atovaquone) and folate synthesis (proguanil). Chloroquine phosphate tablets are supplied in 250 mg and 500 mg strengths, while hydroxychloroquine sulfate tablets are offered in 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, and 400 mg doses; both agents interfere with parasite heme detoxification. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 894
retrieved_docs.content: Atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg Oral Tablet, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg Oral Tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 20 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg Oral Tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg Oral Tablet, pyrimethamine 25 mg Oral Tablet, and tafenoquine 100 mg Oral Tablet are all formulated as oral tablets. These agents span several therapeutic classes—antimalarials (atovaquone/proguanil, chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, mefloquine, pyrimethamine, tafenoquine), an antifungal (griseofulvin), and a corticosteroid (hydrocortisone)—yet they share the same dose‑form, facilitating standardized prescribing, dispensing, and patient administration across diverse treatment indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 278
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy. Antimalarials constitute a pharmacologic class that includes a variety of agents used for the treatment and prevention of Plasmodium infection. Specific drugs classified as antimalarials are acedapson, the fixed‑dose combination artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet, the fixed‑dose combinations atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg oral tablet and atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg oral tablet, chinidin, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg oral tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet, sulfadoxine, and tafenoquine in both 100 mg and 150 mg oral tablet formulations. All of these agents share the common purpose of inhibiting the growth or survival of Plasmodium species, thereby reducing the morbidity and mortality associated with malaria. Understanding the relationships among these antimalarial drugs and their target organism is essential for selecting appropriate therapy, optimizing dosing regimens, and managing resistance in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 42
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a wide array of antimalarial agents that are predominantly formulated as oral tablets, a dosage form that promotes standardized dosing and patient adherence. The antimalarial repertoire includes artemisinin‑based combination therapy (artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet), atovaquone‑proguanil combinations (atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg oral tablet and the lower‑strength atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg oral tablet), quinoline‑based drugs (chloroquine phosphate 250 mg and 500 mg oral tablets, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet), hydroxychloroquine sulfate (available in 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, and 400 mg oral tablets), 8‑aminoquinolines (primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet and tafenoquine 100 mg and 150 mg oral tablets for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax), and antifolate agents (pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet, sulfadoxine, and the fixed‑dose atovaquone‑proguanil combination). Additional antimalarials such as acedapsone, mirincamycin, and chinidin are also recognized, and the trimethoprim‑sulfamethoxazole combination (Kombinace léků trimethoprim a sulfamethoxazol) is occasionally employed for prophylaxis despite its primary antibacterial indication. Non‑antimalarial medications that share the tablet dosage form, including griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet and hydrocortisone tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, and 20 mg oral), further illustrate the commonality of this delivery method across therapeutic classes. Collectively, these agents represent diverse pharmacologic families—artemisinin derivatives, quinolines, 8‑aminoquinolines, antifolates, and others—each with specific indications such as blood‑stage treatment, liver‑stage eradication, long‑acting prophylaxis, or radical cure, thereby guiding patient‑centered malaria management and prevention strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial agent, and a broad range of medications share this classification. Specific antimalarial drugs include the combination tablet of artemether 20 mg and lumefantrine 120 mg, the combination tablet of atovaquone 62.5 mg and proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, chinidin, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg tablets, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg tablets, mirincamycin, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg tablets, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 100 mg tablets. All of these agents are employed to prevent or treat Plasmodium infection, reflecting their shared role in malaria management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a broad class of antimalarial agents that are frequently formulated as oral tablets to promote adherence and standardized dosing. The antimalarial drug class includes traditional quinoline compounds such as chloroquine phosphate (250 mg and 500 mg) and hydroxychloroquine sulfate (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), as well as newer small‑molecule agents like mefloquine hydrochloride (250 mg), primaquine phosphate (26.3 mg), pyrimethamine (25 mg), tafenoquine (100 mg and 150 mg), and the sulfonamide‑based sulfadoxin. Fixed‑dose combinations pair fast‑acting artemisinin derivatives with longer‑acting partners, exemplified by artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg and atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg (with a lower‑strength version of atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil 25 mg). Additional agents listed under the antimalarial umbrella include acedapsone, chinidin, mirincamycin, and the antibacterial combination trimethoprim + sulfamethoxazole, reflecting repurposing of drugs originally developed for other infections. Peptide‑based therapeutics are also represented; the cationic antimicrobial peptide dermaseptin is classified as antimalarial and is biologically related to the magainin‑1 peptide from Xenopus, highlighting interest in membrane‑disruptive mechanisms against Plasmodium. Tafenoquine 150 mg oral tablet is specifically noted for prophylaxis and radical cure of Plasmodium vivax malaria by targeting hepatic stages. The extensive variety of oral tablet formulations—including antimalarials, hydrocortisone (5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg), and griseofulvin (500 mg)—underscores the central role of the tablet dosage form in delivering effective malaria prevention and treatment across diverse patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 517
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial agents. The antimalarial drug class encompasses a diverse range of compounds, each with distinct formulations and therapeutic roles. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial, as are the fixed‑dose oral tablet containing atovaquone 62.5 mg and proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, and the quinoline‑derived agent chinidin. Chloroquine phosphate, commonly supplied as a 250 mg oral tablet, remains a classic antimalarial, while hydroxychloroquine sulfate is available in multiple tablet strengths (100 mg, 300 mg, and 400 mg) and is also employed for malaria treatment and prophylaxis. Sulfadoxine, traditionally used in combination with pyrimethamine, is another antimalarial included in this group. Additionally, mirincamycin—a macrolide‑type compound—has been identified as an antimalarial, and the combination of trimethoprim and sulfamethoxazole, although primarily an antibacterial regimen, possesses activity against Plasmodium and is therefore sometimes considered within the antimalarial spectrum. Collectively, these agents illustrate the breadth of pharmacologic strategies used to prevent and treat malaria, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate therapy based on the specific Plasmodium species, resistance patterns, and patient factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1073
retrieved_docs.content: Atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg oral tablet, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone oral tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, and 20 mg), hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg oral tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet, pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet, and tafenoquine oral tablets (100 mg and 150 mg) are all formulated as tablets for oral administration. Recognizing that each of these medications is delivered in tablet form is important for prescribing practices, dosing accuracy, and patient adherence, especially given the range of therapeutic classes represented—from antimalarials and antifungals to corticosteroids and antiprotozoal agents. The availability of hydrocortisone in multiple tablet strengths further illustrates the need to match specific dosage requirements to individual patient needs.

This uniform tablet dose form facilitates oral administration, standardizes prescribing practices, and supports patient adherence across a range of therapeutic classes, including antimalarials, antifungals, corticosteroids, antiprotozoal agents, and antiretroviral drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1014
retrieved_docs.content: Artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg oral tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone oral tablets in strengths of 5 mg, 10 mg and 20 mg, hydroxychloroquine sulfate oral tablets in strengths of 100 mg and 300 mg, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablet, and pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet are all formulated as solid oral tablets. This common dose‑form indicates that each medication is intended for ingestion through the mouth, providing a convenient and standardized route of administration that facilitates dosing accuracy, patient adherence, and ease of dispensing across a wide range of therapeutic areas, including antimalarial therapy, antifungal treatment, anti‑inflammatory therapy, and antiparasitic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 114
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a broad spectrum of antimalarial agents that are available primarily as oral tablets, facilitating convenient dosing for both treatment and prophylaxis. Synthetic tablet formulations include artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg, atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg, atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg and 500 mg, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg and 400 mg, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg, pyrimethamine 25 mg, tafenoquine 100 mg and 150 mg, sulfadoxine, and the combination of trimethoprim and sulfamethoxazole (occasionally classified as antimalarial). Additional antimalarial substances comprise the synthetic drug Acedapson, the natural product chinidin, and the peptide dermaseptin, which belongs to the cationic antimicrobial peptide family and is structurally related to magainin 1 peptide derived from Xenopus species, underscoring its potential dual antimicrobial and antimalarial activity. Non‑antimalarial medications also employ the tablet dosage form, exemplified by griseofulvin 500 mg (an antifungal) and hydrocortisone tablets (5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg), illustrating the format’s role in standardized prescribing, patient adherence, and pharmacy dispensing across diverse therapeutic classes. Collectively, these agents represent the chemical diversity, dosing options, and mechanistic origins of antimalarial therapy and highlight the central importance of oral tablet formulations in managing Plasmodium infection.
